patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 29-year-old female elementary school teacher. Be cooperative and speak clearly. Start with: “I’ve been feeling really tired for the past couple of weeks, and my eyes look a little yellow again.” If asked about other symptoms, volunteer: skin looks yellow, urine darker than usual, and you get short of breath faster climbing stairs. If asked about history, say this has happened on-and-off since childhood, often after colds or stomach bugs; you’ve been told your spleen was “big” and you feel left-sided fullness/bloating; occasional racing heart when very tired; easy bruising without major bleeding; prior gallstones and mild upper belly/back aches after greasy meals; family history of anemia or spleen removal.

Do not volunteer initially that you started an iron supplement yourself; mention it only if asked about meds/supplements. Be hesitant about weekend alcohol use and missed work—downplay unless directly asked, then admit it briefly. Express worries/misconceptions: “Could this be hepatitis or liver failure?” “Maybe dehydration or lack of sleep?” “Maybe iron deficiency.” If clinician shows empathy, acknowledge it, relax, and share cost concerns about genetic testing, hematology, and frequent labs.

Peripheral smear.value: Stomatocytes 12-15% of RBCs; polychromasia present; no schistocytes

Ektacytometry (osmoscan).value: Pattern consistent with hereditary xerocytosis/stomatocytosis (left-shifted curve, decreased Omin)

Urinalysis.normal_range: Urobilinogen negative/trace; no hemoglobin; no protein

medications: Folic acid 1 mg PO daily
Levonorgestrel/ethinyl estradiol 0.15/0.03 mg PO daily
comorbidities: Cholelithiasis (pigment gallstones) with intermittent biliary colic
confounders: Recent viral upper respiratory infection (2 weeks ago) with transient worsening fatigue and jaundice
Moderate alcohol intake (6-8 drinks/week) which can cause acquired stomatocytosis on smear
Oral contraceptive use (increases baseline venous thromboembolism risk; thrombotic risk can be relevant in some hereditary stomatocytosis phenotypes)
Family history of anemia labeled as 'hereditary spherocytosis' in a parent (misclassification risk)

required_evidence: chronic hemolytic anemia pattern with reticulocytosis
elevated MCHC (dehydrated type) or low/normal MCHC (overhydrated type)
exclude immune hemolysis and other common hemolytic anemias

medications: folic acid 1 mg PO daily
non_pharmacological: hematology referral for confirmatory testing and subtype classification
monitor for hemolysis complications (gallstones, iron overload)

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 62-year-old anxious but clear-speaking woman. Start with: “I keep getting sudden hot flashes where my face turns bright red, and I’ve had off-and-on diarrhea for weeks.” Early, volunteer: sudden red-hot flushing of face/neck, loose stools several times/day that come and go, crampy belly before urgent diarrhea, and feeling wiped out/weak.

If asked, add: episodes a few times daily lasting a couple minutes; sometimes triggered by stress, alcohol, or big meals; heart pounding; wheezing/tight chest during episodes; a few pounds of unintentional weight loss; watery stools more than bloody; lightheaded standing, worse after diarrhea.

Do not offer unless specifically asked: red blotchy non-itchy skin, more short of breath on stairs, ankle puffiness, darker urine on bad diarrhea days.

Resist/feel embarrassed and only admit if asked gently: a few bathroom accidents, lower sex drive, avoiding going out due to bathroom fear.

Express misconceptions: worry about parasites from recent travel/street food; think it might be asthma; worry your immune system is weak due to more colds.

If clinician shows empathy, you relax, thank them, and share more.

Money concerns: say you sometimes skip doses to stretch meds, assume long-acting meds cost more, and think frequent labs/imaging are optional extras you may skip to avoid co-pays.

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis explicitly using the term "insomnia" and distinguishes it from normal short sleep or transient stress-related sleep disruption.

Screens for common contributors and mimics before treating (substance/caffeine/alcohol/nicotine use, medications, pain, depression/anxiety, circadian rhythm disorder, obstructive sleep apnea symptoms, restless legs symptoms).
Provides a clear treatment explanation that must include both "CBT-I" and "sleep hygiene" and sets expectations that CBT-I is first-line and typically most durable.

Discusses key CBT-I components at a high level (stimulus control, sleep restriction/bedtime consolidation, cognitive strategies, relaxation) or offers referral/access options for CBT-I (digital or therapist-based).
Gives specific sleep hygiene recommendations (consistent wake time, limit naps, reduce evening caffeine/alcohol, wind-down routine, bedroom environment, limit screens/light, exercise timing).

Performs required safety checks before prescribing any medication: allergy_check, drug_interaction_check, and contraindication_check; does not prescribe if any critical safety issue is present without addressing it.

If medication is used, uses short-term/lowest effective dose approach, counsels on next-day impairment and avoiding alcohol/other sedatives, and avoids high-risk choices when contraindicated (e.g., benzodiazepines in high fall-risk, untreated OSA, or substance use disorder).
Avoids initiating chronic sedative-hypnotic therapy as first-line without CBT-I and without a plan to reassess and taper/discontinue.
Creates a follow-up expectation (timeframe and goals such as sleep efficiency, total sleep time, daytime function) even if a follow-up tool is not required.

Checks for red flags/instability (syncope, resting hypoxemia, hypotension, signs of right heart failure) and escalates/urgently refers if present, even in an L0 task.

Does not prescribe PAH-specific therapy in this L0 diagnosis-only benchmark; if treatment is discussed, it is framed as general options to be managed by specialists and not issued as a prescription.

Uses clear uncertainty language if confirmatory testing is incomplete (e.g., right heart catheterization) and avoids overclaiming definitive diagnosis without acknowledging standard confirmation.
